coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots derived from fungus for sensing hyaluronic acid and hyaluronidase
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Kuncheng
Liu, Mingxu
Wang, Yingyi
Wang, Shanshan
Miao, Hong
Yang, Liqun
Yang, Xiaoming
coredata.dc:description: Herein, one type of facile-green carbon dots (CDs) derived from the mushroom (fungus) has been successfully prepared with a quantum yield (QY) of nearly 15.3%. Interestingly, not only the precursor of this CDs and the whole synthesis procedure were environmental-friendly, but the CDs also exhibited multiple advantages including excellent photostability, non-toxicity and satisfactory stability. Significantly, sensitively assaying hyaluronic acid (HA) and hyaluronidase (HAase) in a maneuverable way have been further proposed on the basis of HA adsorbed on the surface of CDs by static electric and further digested by HAase. To be specific, the CDs exhibited obviously electrostatic adsorption towards HA, resulting in an effective fluorescence quenching through the aggregations appearing. Again, the enzymatic digestion between HA and HAase occurred, thus an immediately fluorescent recovery appeared once HAase was introduced. Thereby, quantitative evaluation of HAase concentration in a linear range from 0.2UmL−1 to 10000UmL−1 was achieved along with the range from 50 pM to 50μM for HA, and its practicability was subsequently validated by detecting human urine samples, suggesting that the current strategy could broaden the sensing approaches for assaying HA and HAase.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Assaying
Hyaluronic acid
Hyaluronidase

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quenching of CDs by HA
Detections of HAase in urine specimens
Optimizing the synthesis conditions of CDs
Principle for detecting HA and HAase

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of detecting HA and HAase.

(A) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs, Inset: photographs of CDs under daylight (I) and UV light (II); (B) HR-TEM image and DLS analysis of CDs; (C) FTIR spectrum of CDs; (D) XPS of CDs.
(A) Size distributions of CDs, CDs by adding HA, CDs with both HA and HAase; (B) Fluorescence spectra and the corresponding images of CDs, CDs by adding HA, CDs with both HA and HAase.

(A) Effect of different ions and coexisting compounds on the fluorescence intensity for sensing HA; (B) Effect of different ions and coexisting compounds on the fluorescence intensity during assaying HAase.

Fluorescence spectra of CDs in the presence of varied concentrations of HA (A), and HAase (C); Plot of the fluorescence intensity Fluorescence versus the logarithmic concentrations of HA (B), and HAase (D) added.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots derived from fungus for sensing hyaluronic acid and hyaluronidase

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, State Key Laboratory of Silkworm Genome Biology, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Silkworm Genome Biology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: One type of facile-green carbon dots (CDs) originated from the mushroom (fungus) has been successfully prepared. Significantly, sensitively assaying hyaluronic acid (HA) and hyaluronidase (HAase) in a maneuverable way have been further proposed on the basis of HA adsorbed on the surface of CDs by static electric and further digested by HAase.

Herein, one type of facile-green carbon dots (CDs) derived from the mushroom (fungus) has been successfully prepared with a quantum yield (QY) of nearly 15.3%. Interestingly, not only the precursor of this CDs and the whole synthesis procedure were environmental-friendly, but the CDs also exhibited multiple advantages including excellent photostability, non-toxicity and satisfactory stability. Significantly, sensitively assaying hyaluronic acid (HA) and hyaluronidase (HAase) in a maneuverable way have been further proposed on the basis of HA adsorbed on the surface of CDs by static electric and further digested by HAase. To be specific, the CDs exhibited obviously electrostatic adsorption towards HA, resulting in an effective fluorescence quenching through the aggregations appearing. Again, the enzymatic digestion between HA and HAase occurred, thus an immediately fluorescent recovery appeared once HAase was introduced. Thereby, quantitative evaluation of HAase concentration in a linear range from 0.2UmL−1 to 10000UmL−1 was achieved along with the range from 50 pM to 50μM for HA, and its practicability was subsequently validated by detecting human urine samples, suggesting that the current strategy could broaden the sensing approaches for assaying HA and HAase.

ce:section.ce:para: Being a macromolecule of linear polysaccharide along with good water solubility, hyaluronic acid (HA) is composed of repeating d-glucuronic acid and N-acetyl-d-glucosamine. As the major component of the extracellular matrix, HA could regulate the cell adhesion, migration and proliferation [1], and several biological characteristics of the tumor were closely related to HA [2]. Accordingly, exploring simple, rapid and sensitive methods for detecting HA are of great importance. Meanwhile, hyaluronidase (HAase) plays the role as an enzyme that degrades HA specifically by cleaving the internal β-N-acetyl-d-glucosamine linkages of HA, thus increasing the tissue permeability [3]. As been reported, HAase over expresses in the certain patients with cancers (e.g. bladder, colon, prostate, and beyond), and it has been recognized as a new type of tumor marker accordingly [4,5]. In fact, there have existed various methods for HAase detection mainly including turbidimetric, viscometric, zymography, immunoassay, instrumental and chemical methods such as colorimetric [6], fluorescent detections [7] and chemiluminescence-assisted assay. Among the strategies for HAase sensing, the classical methods (e.g. turbidimetric, viscosimetric and colorimetric methods) usually lack sensitivity and selectivity. Though zymography is simple, but it is not suitable for sensitive quantitative analyses. Immunoassays are known as sensitive and selective, but specialized and expensive reagents are necessary. And instrument-based methods are sensitive and precise, but time-consuming and complicated instruments are usually required. Consequently, developing facile and accurate strategies for sensing HAase are still in demand.

Currently, carbon dots (CDs) are emerging as one type of charming nano materials due to their unique properties including stable photoluminescence, tunable excitation and emission wavelength, lack of optical blinking, small size, low toxicity, favorable biocompatibility and satisfactory fluorescent performance [8–10]. Therefore, this kind of material has drawn considerable attentions in the fields of nanobiotechnology, biological labeling, photocatalysis, sensing, biomedicine and delivering drugs and beyond [11–13]. During the past years, two major approaches have been raised for synthesizing carbon dots. One way was known as the top down, consisting of electrochemical oxidation, acidic oxidation, arc discharge and laser ablation [14–17]. For the other way, hydrothermal, microwave, and ultrasonic defined as the bottom up, have been basically applied to prepare carbon dots [18–20]. Compared with conventional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) that are seriously toxic for human health and our environment, carbon dots exhibited fascinating properties and potentiality not only in theoretic but also in practical aspects [21,22]. Especially, numerous carbon dots have been employed to design fluorescent probes towards analysis purposes [23,24] For instance, various carbon dots have served as biosensors for detecting the clinical proteins.

Hereby, we have succeeded to establish a green synthesis of carbon dots by one-step hydrothermal treatment of fungus within short time (6h) (Fig. 1 ). The mechanism for this CDs formation mainly involved polymerization, dehydration and carbonization of the constituents such as saccharides, citric acid, and ascorbic acid in mushroom [25,26]. Besides, the precursor of CDs and the whole synthesis procedure are substantially environment-friendly, so that facilitating their biocompatibility and more extensive applications. Moreover, we developed a label-free fluorimetric assay with high sensitivity for monitoring the levels of both HA and HAase, while the CDs served as the fluorescent sensor and HA as the intermediate. To be specific, the fluorescence of CDs was obviously quenched once HA was introduced, owing to the electrostatic adsorption of HA to the surface of CDs, and thus photoinduced electron transfer (PET) possibly occurring. Subsequently, HA was decomposed by HAase upon adding into HAase, resulting in the recovery of quenched fluorescence.

Mushroom was purchased from Yonghui Supermarket (Chongqing, China). Hyaluronic acid (HA) and hyaluronidase (HAase) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Co. Ltd. Metal ions (e.g. Co2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, and Ba2+), DNase I, RNase I, EXO I and papain were obtained from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4) sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), glacial acetic acid (HAc), phosphoric acid (H3PO4), boric acid (H3BO3), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), sodium chloride (NaCl), methanol, ethanol, acetone, acetic acid, acetonitrile, dimethyl sulfoxide, tetramethylene oxide and methyl chloroform were purchased from Dingguo Changsheng Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Ultrapure water (18.25MΩcm) produced with an Aquapro AWL-0502-P ultrapure water system was employed for all the following experiments.

All fluorescence measurements were performed on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) with an excitation slit set at 5nm band pass and emission at 5nm band pass in 1cm×1cm quartz cell. Meanwhile, UV/Vis absorption spectra were recorded by a Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). The high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were produced using a TECNAI G2 F20 microscope (Portland, America) at 200KV. The size distribution was obtained via a Zetasizer Nano Dynamic light scattering (DLS) instrument (Malvern, England). Elemental and functional group analyses were obtained by ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan), respectively. The quantum yields were obtained by using Absolute PL quantum yield spectrometer C11347 (Hamamatsu, Japan). The powder of CDs was obtained by lyophilization in PiloFD8-4.3V (Charlotte, USA). A Fangzhong pHS-3C digital pH meter (Chengdu, China) was applied to measure the pH values of the aqueous solutions, and a vortex mixer QL-901 (Haimen, China) was used for blending the solutions. The thermostatic water bath (DF-101s) was purchased from Gongyi Instrument Co., Ltd (Gongyi, China).

The green CDs derived from fungus here were synthesized for the first time. All glassware and magnetic stir bars used in this experiment were thoroughly cleaned in freshly prepared aqua regia (HCl/HNO3 3:1, v/v), rinsed in distilled water, and further oven-dried prior to use. Briefly, mushrooms were dried at 80°C for 24h and ground as powders. Again, 0.6g of mushroom powder with 6mL of ultrapure water was added into a 25mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. After heating at 200°C for 6h, the autoclave was cooled to the room temperature. This aqueous solution was centrifuged at 10000rpm for 15min, and the supernatant was collected. Then, the aqueous solution was filtered with 0.22μm filter membrane to remove the larger products. Again, the fluorescent carbon dots were collected by dialysis against deionized water through a dialysis membrane (100 MWCO) for 24h. Finally, the powder of CDs was obtained by lyophilization and further dissolved in ultrapure water at the final concentration of 1μgmL−1. This CDs were stable for 3 months and stored in the dark at 4°C for the further applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of CDs by HA
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 100μL of CDs (1mgmL−1) and 50μL of PBS (20mM, pH=7.4) were successively pipetted into a 1.5-mL vial. Subsequently, 50μL of HA (0.05, 0.5, 2, 5, 20, 70, 100, 500, 2000, 50000nM) was added separately, and diluted to 800μL with Milli-Q purified water. The mixtures was incubated at 37°C for 30min and further subjected to the fluorescence measurements. To evaluate the interference for assaying HA, HAase, DNase I, papain, Co2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, and Ba2+ (5mgmL−1 for each) were introduced to investigate the selectivity of the CDs for HA under the optimal conditions.

At the very beginning, 100μL of CDs (1mgmL−1), 50μL of PBS (20mM, pH=7.4) and HA (50μL, 50μM) were successively pipetted into a 1.5mL vial. Next, 50μL of HAase solutions with varied concentrations (e.g. 0.2, 0.5, 2, 10, 100, 200, 1000, 5000, and 10000UmL−1) were introduced, and further diluted to 750μL with Milli-Q purified water along. Finally, all the above mixtures was incubated to react at 37°C for 30min and subjected to the fluorescence measurements. To evaluate the selectivity of CDs for HAase, DNase I, RNase I, EXO I papain, Co2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, and Ba2+ (5mgmL−1 for each) were introduced for investigating the selectivity of the CDs-HA for HAase with the optimal conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detections of HAase in urine specimens
ce:section.ce:para: Human urine samples from three healthy volunteers were originally collected from Southwest University Hospital (Chongqing, China). For the recovery experiments, impurities of the urine samples were firstly removed by centrifugation (10000rpm, 10min). Then, the supernatant was collected into a 1.5mL vial and adjusted as acidic, and further supplemented with the standard HAase solutions (e.g. 300ng, 3μg, and 30μg). Finally, the urine samples were respectively subjected to this proposed strategy.

To get an insight into the fluorescence mechanism of the CDs described here, a series of characterizations have been successively performed. Basically, the CDs exhibited distinctly a peak maximum at 455nm with blue fluorescence upon 370nm excitation (Fig. 2 A). Simultaneously, the aqueous solution of CDs emitted obvious blue fluorescence (Fig. 2A, photograph II) under UV light (365nm), while appeared as transparent under daylight (Fig. 2A, photograph I). Moreover, the emission peak shifted to longer wavelength and the fluorescence intensity decreased with the excitation wavelengths varying from 370nm to 450nm (Fig. S1, ESI†), suggesting the emissions of the CDs were excitation-dependent. Likewise, the UV–vis absorption spectrum showed a peak at 285nm owing to an n–π* transition of CO (Fig. S2, ESI†). Also, HR-TEM was employed to directly observe the morphology and particle size distributions of the CDs. Specifically, the CDs obtained here existed as a majority population at the size of 2.3nm (Fig. 2B), describing their satisfactory dispersity. For the size distribution analysis, the diameter of CDs existed in the range of 2.3±1.3nm determined by dynamic light scattering (DLS), which showed a satisfactory agreement with that of HR-TEM. Meanwhile, the lattice spacing of 0.21nm was accompanied with the [100] fact of graphitic carbon.

Again, FTIR and XPS were employed to analyze the functional groups and components of the as-prepared CDs in detail. Initially, the surface groups of the synthesized CDs were analyzed by FTIR. In brief, the peak at 3438cm−1 could be ascribed to the characteristic absorption of OH stretching vibration while the CO stretching vibration located at 1678cm−1, revealing the existence of the carboxylic groups (Fig. 2C). Similarly, the peak at 2964cm−1 was attributed to the NH stretching vibration. To elucidate the components of these CDs, XPS survey spectra were provided (Fig. 2D). The XPS surveys of these CDs further confirm the IR data [27]. Three major peaks of C, O and N obviously emerged in the spectrum, suggesting that CDs here were mainly composed of C (58.16%), O (9.26%) and N (30.67%). Moreover, there were four peaks displayed at 284.7eV, 285.8eV, 287.9eV and 292.4eV (Fig. S3A, ESI†), demonstrating the existence of CC, CN, and CO on the surfaces of CDs. Again, three peaks at 398.9eV, 399.8eV, and 401.0eV of the N1s spectrum (Fig. S3B, ESI†) were attributed to the NH, N-(C)3 and CNC bands, respectively. Simultaneously, the O1s spectrum (Fig. S3C, ESI†) exhibited two peaks at 531.2 and 532.3eV, which are attributed to COH, COC and CO groups respectively. Overall, these data indicated that the CDs produced here were equipped with the characteristic functional groups of COOH, OH, NH2 and CO [28]. The above FTIR and XPS results clearly clarified that this CDs consisted of π-conjugated domains and amorphous regions on their surfaces, and the oxidation level of these CDs at the same time [29]. Also, multiple hydroxyl groups of the CDs surfaces resulted in their high polarity and hydrophilicity [30]. In addition, the CDs scarcely imposed toxicity on cells even at a high dose (3mgmL−1) and long incubation time (24h), demonstrating the favorable biocompatibility and potentiating the possibility towards in vivo applications (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizing the synthesis conditions of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Subsequently, a series of experiments were performed to explore the optimal conditions for synthesizing CDs. As revealed in Fig. S5A and B (ESI†), the fluorescence intensity of CDs varied along with the reaction time and temperature, describing that synthesis of CDs depended on the two selected conditions. Therefore, 6h and 200°C served as the optimized conditions for preparing the CDs.

To identify the stability of the CDs for bearing circumstances, further experiments were performed. As revealed, the fluorescent intensities of CDs scarcely exhibited variation along with different concentrations of NaCl, pH and time, demonstrating the favorable stability and tolerance for pH and photobleaching (Fig. S6A–C, ESI†). Furthermore, the as-synthesized CDs could keep stable in the presence of several organic solvents, showing the routine organic solvents exhibited little effect on the current CDs (Fig. S6D, ESI†).

To elucidate the CDs in more details, the powder of CDs was obtained by lyophilization, and showed an obvious fluorescence (right) compared with that of NaCl powder (left) under UV light (Fig. S7A), whereas CDs and NaCl existed as the routine powder under daylight (Fig. S7B). Additionally, this fluorescent powder has been preserved for more than 3 months without marked change, indicating that it may provide the possibility for matching the requirement of commercial purpose. Again, we applied the CDs as the fluorescent dye for staining, and both absorbent cotton and filter paper with staining (right) implied fluorescence under UV light compared with that without staining (left) in Fig. S8A and B . Hence, these photographs described that the fluorescent CDs implied the potential towards more applications and beyond.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle for detecting HA and HAase
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the current CDs could be employed for detecting both HA and HAase, this related sensing mechanisms were explored in detail (Fig. 3 ). In particular, the size of CDs was 2.3±1.3nm, and their average size obviously varied to 19.3±0.8nm while HA was introduced, suggesting the sizes of CDs obviously increased, thus leading to HA combined with CDs. However, their sizes went back to 2.8±1.2nm, indicating the interactions of between CDs and HA disappeared, so that induced a decomposition of HA from the CDs (Fig. 3A and B). Furthermore, the ξ-value of the CDs obviously changed from −18.5±1.2 to 15.3±0.8mV, while pH was adjusted from 7 to 4 (Fig. S9) [31]. The 15.3±0.8mV of CDs were connected with −12.4±1.0mV of HA through electrostatic attraction, forming the ground-state complexes with −6.3±1.2mV. During this process, the excited-state electrons of CDs transferred to HA after excitation and induced the fluorescence quenching, suggesting PET possibly appearing. Upon the addition of HAase, HA was decomposed by HAase from the CDs surfaces, their potential of CDs went back to 10.3±1.5mV, leading to the recovery of the quenched fluorescence [28,32].

For exploring the selectivity of the CDs during the procedures of sensing HA or HAase, the related experiments were performed. Specifically, the fluorescence intensity at 455nm of CDs with HA was obviously quenched. Howbeit, the fluorescence intensities exhibited scarce change by introducing Co2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Ba2+,HAase, DNase I, and papain (5mgmL−1 for each), indicating that the possible coexisted matrix did not interfere with the process of sensing HA (Fig. 4 A). Likewise, the fluorescence showed little change by adding into Co2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, DNase I, RNase I, EXO I, and papain (5mgmL−1 for each), suggesting that the CDs-HA could also selectively assay HAase (Fig. 4B).

Furthermore, the corresponding calibration curves for HA and HAase were built up with the optimal conditions. Particularly, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased along with the logarithmic plots of HA concentrations (e.g. 0.05, 0.5, 2, 5, 20, 70, 100, 500, 2000, and 50000nM) with its correlation coefficient of 0.995. Simultaneously, the detection limit was 0.03nM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (Fig. 5 A and B). Similarly, the fluorescence of CDs recovered as well as the logarithmic of HAase concentrations (e.g. 0.2, 0.5, 2, 10, 100, 200, 1000, 5000, and 10000UmL−1) with a correlation coefficient of 0.994, and the detection limit was 0.1UmL−1 at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (Fig. 5C and D). Taken together, the above results suggested the sensitivity and promising of the CDs probe.

For investigate the practicality of this developed approach, the standard recovery experiments were designed for human urine samples (Table S1). As listed, the recoveries of all samples were obtained as 103.0%, 97.7%, 105.0% respectively, proving little inference from the coexisting substrates of the human urine. Thus, the proposed method may broaden ways for the practical detections of HAase in urine samples.

In summary, we have creatively synthesized one type of green carbon dots derived from fungus. The whole synthesis procedure was facile and environmental-friendly. Consequently, we further designed a label-free assay for monitoring HA and HAase levels, while CDs played the role as the fluorescent signal. In particular, the fluorescence of CDs was obviously quenched once HA was introduced, due to the electrostatic adsorption of HA to the surface of CDs, leading to their fluorescence quenching. In contrast, HA was decomposed from the CDs surfaces by HAase, hence facilitating the recovery of the quenched fluorescence. This strategy showed an acceptable stability for detecting HA and HAase, and covered the broad linear range with the favorable detection limits. Besides, the mechanism for the CDs sensing the two targets is likely due to PET occurring. This contribution may provide a new way for detecting HA or HAase by using the label-free carbon dots.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support by Fundamental Research Funds for Innovative Project on Designing and Screening Drug Candidates of Chongqing (cstc2015zdcy-ztzx120003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.05.086.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hyaluronic acid: targeting immune modulatory components of the extracellular matrix in atherosclerosis
Multifunctional tumor-Targeting nanocarriers based on hyaluronic acid-mediated and pH-sensitive properties for efficient delivery of docetaxel
An upconversion luminescence nanoprobe for the ultrasensitive detection of hyaluronidase
Cationic conjugated polymer/hyaluronan-doxorubicin complex for sensitive fluorescence detection of hyaluronidase and tumor-targeting drug delivery and imaging
One-step facile synthesis of hyaluronic acid functionalized fluorescent gold nanoprobes sensitive to hyaluronidase in urine specimen from bladder cancer patients
Direct detection of hyaluronidase in urine using cationic gold nanoparticles: a potential diagnostic test for bladder cancer
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning sensors, and bioimaging
A fluorescent biosensor based on carbon dots-labeled oligodeoxyribonucleotide and graphene oxide for mercury (II) detection
Distinguish cancer cells based on targeting turn-on fluorescence imaging by folate functionalized green emitting carbon dots
A simple and sensitive fluorescent sensor for methyl parathion based on L-tyrosine methyl ester functionalized carbon dots
Carbon dots rooted agarose hydrogel hybrid platform for optical detection and separation of heavy metal ions
Rational design of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for efficient photoelectrical conversion applications
Facile preparation of low cytotoxicity fluorescent carbon nanocrystals by electrooxidation of graphite
Extraction of electrochemiluminescent oxidized carbon quantum dots from activated carbon
Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
One-step synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles by laser irradiation
Comment on upconversion and downconversion fluorescent graphene quantum dots: ultrasonic preparation and photocatalysis
Carbon dots for copper detection with down and upconversion fluorescent properties as excitation sources
Bifunctional fluorescent carbon nanodots: green synthesis via soy milk and application as metal-free electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction
DNA-carbon dots function as fluorescent vehicles for drug delivery
A carbon dot-based off-on fluorescent probe for highly selective and sensitive detection of phytic acid
Toward structurally defined carbon dots as ultracompact fluorescent probes
Reusable sensor based on functionalized carbon dots for the detection of silver nanoparticles in cosmetics via inner filter effect
Fluorescent carbon nanodots conjugated with folic acid for distinguishing folate-receptor-positive cancer cells from normal cells
On off on fluorescent carbon dot nanosensor for recognition of chromium(VI) and ascorbic acid based on the inner filter effect
Novel and green synthesis of high-fluorescent carbon dots originated from honey for sensing and imaging
Label-free fluorimetric detection of CEA using carbon dots derived from tomato juice
Full-color light-emitting carbon dots with a surface-state-controlled luminescence mechanism
Tunable photoluminescence across the entire visible spectrum from carbon dots excited by white light
Luminescence turn-on/off sensing of biological iron by carbon dots in transferrin
Exciplex formation in bimolecular photoinduced electron-transfer investigated by ultrafast time-resolved infrared spectroscopy

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xiaoming Yang respectively obtained his B.S. degree in 2004 and Ph. D. degree in 2009 at Sichuan University, China. During 2007 to 2009, he stayed at Purdue University as a Joint Ph.D. for pursuing his study in the field of biochemistry. Since 2010, he has been serving as a full professor in College of Pharmaceutical Sciences of Southwest University, China. His research interests mainly focus on the functional carbon dots, metal nanoclusters and their related applications as the fluorescent sensors.
